did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Artificial Intelligence, specifically Deep Learning and Machine Learning techniques, to obtain results in dermatology. The proposed method involves an Automated Skin Cancer Detection system using Radial Basis Function Networks (RBFNs) for classification, Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs) for preprocessing (noise removal and dimensionality reduction), and Fuzzy Clustering Segmentation for lesion delineation. The study evaluates these AI models on 4500 dermoscopic images, achieving an accuracy of 92.7% for classification and reporting segmentation effectiveness of 79%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing an automated skin cancer detection system using Radial Basis Function Networks (RBFNs), Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs), and Fuzzy Clustering. It details specific methodologies, feature extraction techniques, and reports new empirical results, including a classification accuracy of 92.7% on 4500 dermoscopic images, which distinguishes it from a review paper.

title: Early Stage Skin cancer Diagnosis with RBFNs: A Feature – based Segmentation and Classification using Deep Learning
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on Sustainable Communication Networks and Application (ICSCN-2025)
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated early-stage skin cancer diagnosis system utilizing Radial Basis Function Networks (RBFNs) for classification, supported by Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs) for preprocessing and Fuzzy Clustering for segmentation. The methodology involves extracting features such as lesion irregularity, coloration, and diameter from 4500 dermoscopic images. The system was evaluated against conventional classifiers, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Support Vector Machines (SVMs). The proposed RBFN-based approach achieved a classification accuracy of 92.7%, outperforming the compared methods. Additionally, the segmentation component demonstrated an effectiveness rate of 79%. The results indicate that the hybrid framework improves diagnostic precision and reduces reliance on manual inspection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on automated skin cancer detection. The classification task distinguishes between malignant (positive/cancerous) and benign (negative/non-cancerous) lesions. The system was evaluated on 4500 dermoscopic images to diagnose skin cancer.

The binary classification output includes 'Negative' for non-cancerous cells and 'benign' lesions, serving as the comparator to malignant cases. The system distinguishes between healthy and cancerous tissues.

all_task_types.details: The paper describes an automated skin cancer detection system using Radial Basis Function Networks (RBFNs) for classification. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories: 'Positive' (malignant/cancerous cells) or 'Negative' (benign/non-cancerous cells). The classification is based on a decision rule where the output score is compared to a threshold (ŷ = 1 if y(x) ≥ θ for malignant, ŷ = 0 if y(x) < θ for benign). The primary evaluation metric reported is classification accuracy (92.7%).

The paper employs Fuzzy Clustering Segmentation (Fuzzy C-Means) to isolate the lesion region from the background skin. This is described as a preprocessing/feature extraction step rather than the primary predictive task, with a reported segmentation effectiveness of 79%.

datasets_used.name: Dermoscopic Image Collection
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermoscopic images used for the automated skin cancer detection system. The specific source, public benchmark name, or private institution is not explicitly stated in the provided text, though it is described as a dataset of dermoscopic images.

use.training: Used for training the Radial Basis Function Networks (RBFNs) and other models.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the classification accuracy (92.7%) and segmentation effectiveness (79%).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (92.7%) and segmentation effectiveness (79%) compared against conventional classifiers (CNNs, SVMs) without statistical significance testing.

Classification.notes: The proposed RBFN method surpassed conventional classifiers such as CNNs and SVMs with an accuracy of 92.7%.

Segmentation.notes: Fuzzy Clustering Segmentation demonstrated an effectiveness rate of 79%.

Task-Specific Performance (Proposed vs E.notes: Table 1 compares existing methods with proposed segmentation and classification. The proposed model improved detection accuracy by 20% for boundary, 17% for skin pigmentation, and 15% for diameter estimation compared to conventional methods. The conclusion also mentions 'enhanced predictive precision at 87%' and 'Five percent accuracy' alongside the 79% segmentation effectiveness.

architecture_used.input_size: M x N x 3 (RGB dermoscopic images)

components: Preprocessing: Bilateral Filter for noise removal, Normalization to [0,1] range, Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs) for dimensionality reduction
Feature Extraction: Border irregularity (B), Asymmetry (As), Diameter (D), Mean RGB color features (Ck)
Classification: RBFN with Gaussian kernel functions in hidden layer
architecture_details.hidden_layer: Gaussian kernel functions: hj(x) = exp( − ||x − cj||² / (2σj²) )
architecture_details.output_layer: Linear combination: yk(x) = Σ wjk · hj(x)
architecture_details.decision_rule: ŷ = 1 if y(x) ≥ θ (malignant), ŷ = 0 if y(x) < θ (benign)

hyperparameters.rbm_hidden_neurons: Sufficient hidden neurons provided for desired accuracy (specific count not reported)
hyperparameters.threshold_theta: Threshold for classification decision (value not reported)

preprocessing_techniques: Noise removal using a Bilateral Filter
Noise removal using a Median Filter
Normalization of pixel values to the [0,1] range
Dimensionality reduction using Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs)
Dimensionality reduction using Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
Fuzzy Clustering Segmentation (Fuzzy C-Means) to isolate lesion regions from background
conclusions: The proposed automated skin cancer detection system, utilizing Radial Basis Function Networks (RBFNs) combined with Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs) for preprocessing and Fuzzy Clustering for segmentation, achieved a classification accuracy of 92.7% on 4500 dermoscopic images.
The RBFN-based classification method outperformed conventional classifiers, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Support Vector Machines (SVMs).
The segmentation component demonstrated an effectiveness rate of 79%.
The proposed method improved detection accuracy compared to conventional methods by 20% for border detection, 17% for skin pigmentation analysis, and 15% for diameter estimation.
The hybrid framework enhances predictive precision and reduces reliance on manual inspection for distinguishing between cancerous and non-cancerous tissues.

The study acknowledges limitations including challenges in managing various diagnostic scenarios, insufficient data sizes, unbalanced samples, and the need for rapid implementation under constrained resources.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges significant limitations including insufficient data sizes, unbalanced samples, and challenges in managing various diagnostic scenarios.
The paper notes that rapid implementation under constrained resources is a hurdle for the proposed system.
The authors highlight that interpretability, fairness, and privacy problems are not well-addressed in current AI for dermoscopy, implying these are critical gaps for clinical deployment.
The dataset source is not explicitly identified as a standard public benchmark (e.g., ISIC), raising potential concerns about external validation and generalizability.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance comparisons against CNNs and SVMs rely solely on accuracy metrics.
The segmentation effectiveness is reported as 79%, which may be considered moderate for clinical-grade lesion delineation.

The paper mentions 'enhanced predictive precision at 87%' and 'Five percent accuracy' in the conclusion, which are vague or potentially contradictory to the primary 92.7% classification accuracy metric, requiring clarification.

The system relies on handcrafted features (border irregularity, coloration, diameter) combined with RBFNs, which may limit its ability to capture complex deep features compared to end-to-end deep learning models.
